\‘iLH1
(ii A U
FAIR Po[ITIcAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
January 9,
1996
David J. Aleshire
Rutan
& Tucker
611 Anton Boulevard,
Suite 1400
Post Office Box
1950
Costa Mesa,
California
92628—1950
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95—352
Dear Mr. Aleshire:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”)
1 as they pertain to Palm Springs City Mayor
2 Will
Kleindienst.
QUESTIONS
1.
May Mayor Kleindienst participate in decisions that may
affect his spouse’s escrow business and clients of the business?
2.
Do the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Act
prohibit Mayor Kleindienst from participating in the following
City of Palm Springs decisions:
a.
Settlement negotiations in litigation challenging the
Environmental Impact Report concerning the city’s airport;
b.
Implementation of a multi-million dollar program to
install noise insulation within approximately 200 residences
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
2
Councilmember Will Kleindienst was elected Mayor of the City of
Palm Springs on November 7,
1995.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-95—352
Page
2
located within the
65 decibel
CNEL 1999 noise contour around the
city’s airport;
and
c.
Rezoning of certain property from Residential to Office!
Professional in response to the Noise Study recommendations.
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Mayor Kleindienst may not participate in decisions in
which it is reasonably foreseeable that his spouse’s escrow
business or sources of income to the escrow business,
including
individual buyers and sellers of properties,
may be affected in a
material manner.
2.
The mayor must abstain from participating in specific
decisions as discussed below.
a.
It is reasonably foreseeable that some of the litigation
decisions,
including settlement negotiations, will have a
financial effect on the escrow business or sources of income to
the business.
Therefore, if this is the case, the mayor must
abstain from participating in litigation decisions if the business
or sources of income to the business will be materially affected
by the decisions.
This determination must be made on a
case—by—
case basis.
b.
It is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning
the selection of the homes to benefit from the noise insulation
program will have a financial effect on the property owners
selected.
Thus, the mayor must abstain from participating in
decisions concerning the selection process if sources of income to
the mayor will be materially affected by these decisions.
c.
Similarly, it is reasonably foreseeable that rezoning
decisions will have the effect of either increasing or decreasing
property values.
Therefore, the mayor must abstain from
participating in such decisions if the property owners who are
sources of income to the mayor will be materially affected by the
decisions.
FACTS
From the information you have provided,
we have summarized
the facts which are relevant to your request.
City of Palm Springs Mayor Will Kleindienst is an architect
by occupation.
However,
Mr. Kleindienst has given up his
architectural practice.
The mayor’s wife is the sole owner of the
Escrow Connection,
an escrow company within the City of Palm
Springs.
The Escrow Connection is a community property asset.
The Escrow Connection’s fees are generated in accordance with
this formula:
$175 plus
$2 per $1,000 of the purchase price from


Our File No.
A—95--352
Page
3
both the buyer and the seller
(for a $100,000 purchase price, fees
would be $375 for the buyer and $375 for the seller).
The escrow agent is not a broker.
The escrow agent does not
find the buyer or seller,
does not set the price of property,
and
does not negotiate the terms of the transaction.
Typically, the
escrow agent is recommended by one of the brokers.
The escrow
agent enters into
a contractual arrangement with the buyer and the
seller to carry out the terms of the contract between the buyer
and the seller,
as negotiated by those parties.
The city council,
with Mayor Kleindienst abstaining,
in May
1995 approved the Palm Springs Airport Master Plan,
Noise Study,
and Environmental Impact Report,
and made several decisions
regarding applications for federal funding for the Airport Project
(collectively the “Airport Project”).
Following this approval,
a
referendum petition was submitted,
and the matter of approval of
the Master Plan was placed on the November 7,
1995, ballot.
The
ballot measure was approved by the voters.
Future Airport Project decisions
3 will include:
(1)
settlement negotiations concerning the litigation challenging
approval of the Environmental Impact Report
(the “EIR”);
(2)
implementation of a multi—million dollar program to install
noise insulation for approximately 200 residences within the
65
decibel
CNEL 1999 noise contour around the airport;
and
(3) the
rezoning of certain property from Residential to Office!
Professional in response to the Noise Study recommendations.
Including the Escrow Connection, there are some 20 escrow
companies active within the Palm Springs/Cathedral City area.
A
total of 1,442 escrows closed in the Palm Springs/Cathedral City
area from August 1,
1994, to August 1,
1995.
Of this number,
750
or
52% were handled by the Escrow Connection.
The Escrow
Connection collected a gross total of $879,761 for the escrows.
A total of 5,043 properties are located within 2,500 feet of
the airport.
During the August 1,
1994
- August 1,
1995, period,
136 escrows closed within 2,500 feet of the airport.
During that
time, the Escrow Connection handled 36 or 26% of the escrows
within the 2,500 foot radius.
These airport area escrows
generated $31,299 in gross revenues for the Escrow Connection
during this period.
Of the Escrow Connection’s total Palm
Springs/Cathedral City area business,
3.13% stemmed from
properties within 2,500 feet of the airport.
You have inquired about decisions concerning the preparation of
a new master plan, if the master plan before the voters was
defeated.
The ballot measure was approved by the voters on
November 7,
1995.
Therefore, the issue is moot.


Our File No.
A—95—352
Page
4
With respect to the effect of the Airport Project on real
property values, the EIR for the Airport Master Plan addressed the
subject in response to comments received suggesting that property
values would decline.
Although the EIR conceded that airports
with noise impacts can depress property values,
due to the
mitigation measures imposed on this project, the EIR forecast no
significant noise impacts resulting from the project.
Based upon
this lack of impact,
the EIR projected no change in property
values.
To look in more detail at the effect on property values of
the decision to approve the Airport Project, the city commissioned
an appraiser to specifically examine the effect on land values
within 2,500 feet of the airport
(the “Study Area”) before and
after the decision to expand the airport was announced.
The
announcement was made on May 17,
1995.
The appraiser analyzed
single-family residence sales during three time periods:
(1)
the
period from November 17,
1993, to October 17,
1994
(up to
6 months
prior to the announcement of the decision); November 18,
1994, to
May 17,
1995
(6 months prior to the decision;
and May 18,
1995, to
July 20,
1995
(2 months following the decision).
The appraiser
compared the Study Area to the city generally,
and compared it to
a control area with housing priced in the same category (“Control
Area”).
Among other things,
the study showed the following:
1.
For all sales,
between the first and second time
periods
(up to
6 months before the announcement of the decision),
average sale prices per square foot increased in the City of Palm
Springs by 24%,
decreased by
7% in the Study Area,
and decreased
by
10% in the Control Area.
Between the second and third periods
(from the date of the decision to
2 months later), there was a
2%
decrease in sales in the City of Palm Springs,
a
1% decrease in
the Study Area,
and a
16% decrease in the Control Area.
The appraisal report stated:
Examination of the results from the third time
period (5/15/95 to 7/20/95)
reveals a larger
decrease in average market prices in the control
area than the subject.
I recognize that the sample
size used to compute this decrease in the control
area is small because a smaller market is being
measured for only a brief time period.
There is
consequence in noticing that while both markets
moved downward the control area appears to have
[sic]
suffered more than the subject area.
If the announcement of the Airport Master Plan
were negatively influencing marJct values in the
Airport vicinity the average prices should be
decreasing more in the subject area than in the
control area.
The opposite is the case.


Our File No.
A—95—352
Page
5
2.
For all sales in the Study Area,
excluding new home
sales
(5% of the total sales),
between the first and second time
periods, the average sale prices per square foot increased by 2%.
By contrast,
when “all sales” were analyzed there was a decrease
of
7% as shown above.
Between the second and third periods, there
was an increase of
1% for sales in the Study Area, also excluding
new home sales,
compared to a decrease of
1% when “all sales” in
the Study Area were analyzed.
The appraiser concluded that it
appears that the submarket (sales excluding new homes)
is stable
with respect to prices in the Study Area through the time periods
studied.
3.
A comparison of sales of property of more than $50,000
and less than $250,000 revealed that the average price per square
foot in the Study Area increased by
6% and decreased by
1% in the
City of Palm Springs between the first and second time periods.
Between the second and third time periods, the average price in
the Study Area increased by 2.7% and decreased by 3.6% in the City
of Palm Springs.
According to the analysis,
the appraiser opined that the
reason that the average price per square foot of subject submarket
increased while the average in the city submarket decreased is
that the subject submarket units were less expensive than those in
the city.
It was also the appraiser’s opinion that current market
conditions favor the sale of more affordable single family units.
He concluded that “for the times specified in this report,
that the announcement of the Airport Master Plan to expand is
having no measurable impact on the market values of single family
residences” within 2,500 feet of the Palm Springs Airport.
ANALYSIS
I.
Conflicts of Interests Generally
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making,
or using their official position to
influence governmental decisions in which an official knows or has
reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
Mayor
Kleindienst is a public official subject to Section 87100.
(Section 82048.)
The conflict-of-interest analysis under the Act is a four-
part test:
(1)
A public official must be participating in a
governmental decision,
(2)
and it must be reasonably foreseeable
that,
(3)
the decision will have a material financial effect,
(4) distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on
the official,
a member of the official’s immediate family, or on
any one of five statutorily identified economic interests of the
official.


Our File No.
A—95—352
Page
6
A.
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that an official has an economic
interest in a decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if
the decision will affect the official,
or a member of the
official’s immediate family,
or:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect investment
or interest means any investment or interest owned by
the spouse or dependent child of a public official,
by
an agent on behalf of a public official,
or by a
business entity or trust in which the official, the
official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own
directly, indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater.
Section 87103(a)
—
(d).
The facts in your request suggest two economic interests
which may be affected by the mayor’s participation in airport
decisions.
The Escrow Connection:
First,
Mayor Kleindienst has an
investment interest in his spouse’s escrow business totaling


Our File No.
A-95-352
Page
7
$1,000 or more.
Pursuant to Section 87103,
an investment
4
interest held by a spouse of an official is considered an indirect
investment interest of the official.
Therefore, the mayor may not
participate in any decision that will have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on the escrow business in which his
spouse holds the investment.
Sources of Income:
Moreover,
Mayor Kleindienst has a
community property interest in his spouse’s income.
5
(Section
82030.)
Therefore,
any person or business that has made any
payment to his spouse in the past 12 months is a potential source
of income to the mayor for the purposes of Section 87103(c).
Income also includes a pro rata share of any income of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns
directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a lO interest or greater.
(Section 82030.)
Mayor Kleindienst’s spouse wholly owns her
business.
Therefore, if his spouse received income from a client
of $500 or more, half of the income would be attributed to Mayor
Kleindienst and he would have an economic interest in the client
of the business as a source of income.
You have asked whether the purchasers and sellers of real
property are considered sources of income to the escrow business.
You note that the business is paid as a result of referrals by the
brokers; it is not initially chosen by the buyer or seller.
The Commission has developed special rules regarding the
attribution of income, depending on the facts and ciràumstances
surrounding the payment.
For example, the general rule with
respect to an official’s income as a subcontractor is that the
contractor is a source of income to the subcontractor,
even though
the subcontractor is paid with funds that can be traced to the
contractor’s client.
(Cady Advice Letter,
No.
A—94-294.)
This is
because the contractor independently makes the decision to hire
the official and is not directed by the client to hire the
official.
(Harron Advice Letter,
No.
1-86-027;
Schectman Advice
Letter,
No.
A—87-031.)
The escrow business situation may be analogous to that of a
subcontractor relationship if:
(1) the real estate brokers
independently initiate the various financial relationships with
“Investment” means any financial interest in or security issued
by a business entity,
including but not limited to common stock,
preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt instruments and
any partnership or other ownership interest owned directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official,
or his or her
immediate family.
(Section 82034.)
“Income” as used above,
means a payment received,
including but
not limited to any salary,
wage advance, dividend, interest, rent,
proceeds from any sale,
or gifts.


Our File No.
A-95—352
Page
8
the escrow business;
(2)
the real estate brokers have the
discretion to hire the Escrow Connection or some other escrow
company;
and
(3)
the real estate brokers provide direction where
necessary.
(Cady Advice Letter,
supra.)
This would be the case
despite the fact that the escrow business is paid from the funds
provided by the seller and/or the buyer.
It does not appear that the real estate brokers are
contractors who have an independent relationship with the Escrow
Connection as discussed above.
The buyers or sellers select the
business for the escrow work when they accept the recommendation
of the broker.
They are not required to use the Escrow
Connection.
Moreover, they provide direction concerning the
transactions and enter into a contractual relationship with the
Escrow Connection concerning the payments to the Escrow
Connection.
Therefore, the buyers and sellers appear to be
sources of income to the business.
We have also advised that where a law firm refers clients to
a public official’s
law firm,
and the public official receives a
percentage of the total fees billed and collected for the services
performed by the public official’s firm,
both the firm referring
the clients and the clients are jointly responsible for the income
received by the public official’s firm.
Consequently,
we
concluded that under that fact pattern, both the firm referring
the clients and the clients are the source of the full amount of
income to the public official.
(Schenk Advice Letter,
Our File
No.
1-90-460.)
Similarly, under your facts,
we believe both the
buyers and sellers , the real estate brokers could be considered
sources of income to the Escrow Connection.
6
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions
regarding the airport will have a material financial effect on the
Escrow Connection, its clients,
or real estate brokers who are
sources of income to the business,
Mayor Kleindienst must
disqualify himself from making, participating in making, or
attempting to influence such decisions.
Where any one economic
interest of the mayor will be foreseeably and materially affected
by a decision, he may not participate in the decision.
6
Also, under specified circumstances,
a person may be a source
of income to a public official who receives commission income for
a specific transaction.
(Regulation 18704.3.)
By its express
terms, Regulation 18704.3 applies to insurance brokers or agents,
real estate brokers or agents, travel agents or salespersons,
and
stockbrokers or retail or wholesale salespersons.
However,
we
have applied Regulation 18704.3 to a public official who was a
loan officer of a mortgage company and who received commission
income for each real estate transaction in which he participated.
(Lingenfelter,
Our File No.
1-91-262.)
Therefore, consistent with
this analysis,
individual buyers and sellers in connection with a
real estate transaction could be deemed sources of income pursuant
to this regulation as well.


Our File No.
A-95-352
Page
9
B.
Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community
Development Commission
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 938;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
The ultimate test is whether the element
of foreseeability is present to the point that the official’s
“unqualified devotion to his public duty” might be impaired.
(Thorner,
supra; People v.
Darby
(1952)
114 Cal.App.2d 4l2.)
Under the Thorner standard, if there is no indication that
city decisions pertaining to the airport will foreseeably affect
the mayor’s economic interests,
he may participate in the
decisions.
However, this does not appear to be the case.
With respect to the questions you have presented,
the
“foreseeability analysis” must be applied to determine:
(1)
whether it is foreseeable that the Escrow Connection’s share
of the market will be affected by potential litigation concerning
the EIR;
(2)
whether other decisions may increase or decrease
property values, thereby reducing the potential income to the
business;
(3)
whether it is foreseeable that the decisions will
affect real estate brokers, buyers,
and sellers who have been
sources of income to the Escrow Connection in the preceding 12
months;
and
(4)
whether any decisions related to the airport will
increase or decrease property development or turnover so as to
affect Escrow Connection’s gross income.
In order to facilitate the “foreseeability” analysis,
we have
discussed “foreseeability” generally below and each decision
separately.
The Business:
You have provided an appraisal report concerning the effect
of the announcement of the decision to expand the airport on
properties within 2,500 feet of the airport, the City of Palm
Springs as a whole and a Control Area.
Generally, it appears that
the study showed that the announcement of the decision has not had
the immediate effect of negatively impacting the sales prices of
The Thorner Opinion (supra)
concerned requests for water
variances for property slated for development.
The Commission
concluded that such decisions would foreseeably affect business
entities that contract with or supply goods to developers if the
business entities had bid on a developer’s project, were preparing
to bid on the project,
or were likely to supply goods.


Our File No
A-95--352
Page
10
properties within 2,500 feet of the airport.
By contrast,
some of
the data suggest that some positive effects may have resulted,
when comparing the data to sales within the City of Palm Springs
and the Control Area..
8
We would agree, that the fact that the property values in the
area will increase or decrease by the Airport Project is
insufficient alone to find that it is foreseeable that the mayor’s
economic interest in the business will be affected.
(Mattas
Advice Letter,
No.
A—94-l73.)
For example,
in the past we have
advised that where a real estate broker would renounce any
business in the area where the property values would be enhanced,
it would not be foreseeable that decisions affecting the area
would affect the official’s business.
(Libow Advice Letter
No.
1—91—461.)
By contrast,
where a councilmember indicated he may pursue
business in the area in the future,
we concluded, consistent with
Thorner (supra), that it is foreseeable that the councilmember’s
business would be financially affected by the decision.
9
(Mattas
Advice Letter, supra.)
As the Escrow Connection plans to continue
to do business in the area, this rationale would appear to apply
to your facts as well.
8
We also note that the appraisal commissioned by the city is of
limited value for the purpose of the conflict-of-interest analysis
concerning the specific decisions you have posed.
For example,
the determination of statistical trends regarding property values
as a whole over a limited period of time
(two months following the
announcement of the city’s decision to expand the airport)
does
not provide information as to whether a specific property owned by
a client of the Escrow Connection will experience an increase or
decrease in property values, or whether such an increase or
decrease will be material.
Moreover,
in Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development
Corn.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983, the appellate court affirmed a trial
court’s conclusion that a redevelopment plan amendment “had a
foreseeable material effect on Mr.
Santangelo’s income as a
realtor,
since a realtor’s income is based on a percentage of the
value of property sold,
and it was reasonably foreseeable that the
redevelopment plan would increase property values in the
redevelopment area.
Appellants point to evidence that in his
prior experience as a realtor,
Mr. Santangelo dealt primarily with
residential property,
had only one or two listings in the
redevelopment area and no sales in the redevelopment area.
The
trial court could discount appellants’ evidence,
in light of other
evidence that Santangelo’s prior real estate practice was at
another location outside of either redevelopment area,
he had just
recently acquired the real property in the new redevelopment area
in April 1984,
and the Century 21 real estate franchise in May
1984,
and his firm did not actively avoid listings in either
redevelopment area.”
(Id. at p.
990
[emphasis added].)


Our File No.
A-95-352
Page 11
In addition,
the Escrow Connection’s share of the market in
the Palm Springs/cathedral City area is 52%,
and 26% of the market
of the real property within 2,500 feet of the airport.
Therefore,
despite substantial competition, it holds a predominant share of
the market and it is foreseeable that airport decisions in general
would have a financial effect on the escrow company.
(Haile
Advice Letter,
No.
A—91-043.)
For example,
a positive effect in
property values within 2,500 feet of the airport may be correlated
to a negative effect on market sales within the City of Palm
Springs/Cathedral City area.
Expansion of the city’s airport may
ensure future development and growth and encourage tourism,
leading to additional real property transactions and increased
business for the Escrow Connection.
Thus, the effects of the
various decisions on the entire market is relevant to this
inquiry.
We have also advised that a decision that would affect all
development in the jurisdiction,
such as the ordinance that limits
building permits,
would foreseeably affect fl the mortgage
companies in the jurisdiction.
(Siegler Advice Letter,
No.
A—92—508.)
Similarly,
absent conclusive data that the airport
decisions would have no effect on real estate brokers and escrow
companies, the airport decisions could foreseeably financially
affect them.
While you believe that it is not likely that the
Airport Project will result in significantly increased or
decreased land values,
or a marked increase or decrease in the
sale of property to such an extent that the Escrow Connection will
be impacted by $10,000 in escrow fees,
such facts weigh in
consideration of whether a financial effect is material,
not
foreseeability.
A similar reasoning would apply to real estate
brokers who are sources of income to the Escrow Connection.
Finally, the income to the business is set up to constitute a
base amount
($175)
plus
$2 of each $1,000 of the sale price from
both the buyer and the seller.
Thus,
any change in property
values will directly impact the dollar amount received by the
business from a specific transaction.
Therefore, it is our opinion that it is reasonably
foreseeable that many decisions concerning the airport will affect
the mayor’s business interests.
Sources of Income:
With respect to effects on sources of income to the business,
please note that the probability that the Escrow Connection will
do no more than one escrow from the same source in the future is
not the focus of the foreseeability analysis.
If a source of
income has provided the Escrow Connection $500 or more within the
previous
12 months,
a potentially disqualifying economic interest
exists.


Our File No. A-95-352
Page
12
1.
Litigation Decisions
You have asked whether the mayor may participate in
settlement negotiations concerning a challenge of the city’s
Environmental Impact Report.
You have provided no facts
concerning the specific litigation decisions or who has initiated
litigation challenging the EIR.
Therefore,
we provide you only
general guidance concerning litigation decisions that may give
rise to a conflict.
For purposes of our analysis,
we assume that
other elements of the airport expansion plan which may affect
property values,
such as,
increased traffic in areas surrounding
the airport,
changes in character and use of adjoining
neighborhoods may also be challenged.
Presumably, the mayor’s potential participation in litigation
decisions could include choices involving litigation strategy,
settlement of legal claims,
and payment of attorneys’ fees.
The
mayor may not make, participate in the making or influence any of
these decisions, if they will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on the Escrow Connection or his sources
of income.
According to your facts, the EIR,
among other things,
analyzed whether the Airport Project would have noise impact which
could depress property values.
The EIR forecast no significant
noise impact resulting from the project,
and based on this lack of
impact, the EIR projected no change in property values.
Therefore, because the litigation involves a challenge relating to
the findings of the EIR report concerning noise impacts and the
concomitant effect on property values, the outcome of the
litigation is substantially likely to affect the premise upon
which a conflict—of—interest analysis is based, i.e. whether there
is any effect on property values caused by the airport expansion.
Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that the Escrow Connection
and clients
(the mayor’s sources of income)
who own homes in the
noise impact area could be financially affected by the litigation
decisions, including settlement negotiations.
However,
each
litigation decision must be analyzed separately in reaching a
determination on this issue.
For example,
a purely procedural
decision,
such as stipulating to a date for a hearing,
would
probably have no foreseeable financial effect on the mayor’s
economic interests.
(Woodhead Advice Letter,
No.
1-91—266.)
Also, if the mayor is a named defendant in the litigation,
he
may not participate in any decision which will result in either
his or his immediate family’s personal expenses,
income, assets
(other than interests in real property),
or liabilities increasing
or decreasing by $250 or more in a
12—month period.
(Regulation
18702.1(a)(4).)
Thus,
for example, if the decision involved a
determination as to whether the city would be liable for payment
of the attorney’s fees and costs,
incurred by the mayor, it would
be reasonably foreseeable that the decision would affect him
personally,
and he could not participate in that decision.


Our File No.
A—95—352
Page
13
2.
Noise Insulation Program
One of the decisions concerns implementation of a multi—
million dollar program to insulate 200 homes from certain noise
levels.
The installment of such noise insulation would result in
an improvement to each beneficiary’s property.
Therefore, it is
reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the selection of
the homes to benefit from the program will have a financial effect
on the property owners.
Therefore, the mayor must abstain from
participating in such decisions if the property owners who are
sources of income to the mayor will be materially affected by
these decisions.
3.
Rezoning of Property from Residential to Office!
Professional
Similarly, it is reasonably foreseeable that rezoning
decisions will have the effect of either increasing or decreasing
property values.
Therefore, the mayor must abstain from
participating in such decisions if the property owners who are
sources of income to the mayor will be materially affected by the
decisions.
C.
Materiality
Mayor Kleindienst is required to disqualify himself from the
decisions only if the foreseeable effect on his economic interests
is also material.
1.
Economic Interests Directly Involved in the Decision
If Mayor Kleindienst’s economic interests are directly
involved in a decision, then Regulation 18702.1 applies to
determine materiality.
A person or business entity is directly involved in a
decision before an official’s agency when that person or entity,
either personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in,
or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance,
renewal,
approval,
denial or revocation
of any license, permit,
or other entitlement to,
or
contract with, the subject person or business


Our File No.
A-95-352
Page
14
entity.
Regulation 18702.1(b).
Therefore, if the escrow business or a client of the business
is directly involved in a decision before the city as discussed
above, the effect of the decision is deemed material and his
disqualification is required.
(Regulation 18702.1.)
This may be
the case with respect to some of the zoning and noise insulation
application decisions.
2.
Economic Interests Indirectly Involved in the Decision
The litigation decisions would affect the mayor’s interests
indirectly.
In that case, it must be determined if an effect is
material under Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6.
Where an
individual who is a source of income is indirectly affected by a
decision,
the applicable regulation is Regulation 18702.6.
Where the Escrow Connection or a client of the business which
is a source of income is a business entity indirectly affected by
the decisions, the applicable regulation for determining the
appropriate materiality standard is Regulation 18702.2.
If a
decision will affect the business in the amounts set forth in
Regulation 18702.2,
the effect of the decision is material.
According to the information you have provided,
subdivision
(g)
of Regulation 18702.2 would apply.
Regulation 18702.2(g)
provides:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
Therefore,
Mayor Kleindienst must assess the potential effect
of each litigation decision regarding the airport upon this
business interest.
Among the factors to be considered in making
this evaluation is whether the decisions will result in increased
or decreased commissions to the business in the amount of $10,000
or more.
(Caviglia Advice Letter,
No.
1—94—209; Nelson Advice
Letter,
No.
1-92-457.)
If he determines that the effect of such
decisions will be material,
he must disqualify himself from
participating in such decisions.


Our File No.
A-95-352
Page 15
We understand that the airport issue is an extremely complex
matter,
and that it is difficult to determine whether the effect
of the decision is material.
We sympathize with your dilemma, but
unfortunately,
we are unable to provide you with any specific
guidance in determining whether the effect of any particular
decision on Mayor Kleindienst is material.
10
We generally advise
public officials who are faced with such decisions that they must
make a good faith effort to ascertain the magnitude of the effect
that may result from a specific decision.
(Biondo Advice Letter,
No.
1—90—071.)
D.
Public Generally
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision is material, disqualification is required
only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally.
(Section 87103.)
For the “public generally”
exception to apply,
a decision must affect the official’s
interests in substantially the same manner as it would affect a
significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)
I hope we have been of assistance to you.
If you have
further questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LM: ak
10
In issuing advice, the Commission is not a finder of fact.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)

Mayor Kleindienst may not participate in decisions in
which it is reasonably foreseeable that his spouse’s escrow
business or sources of income to the escrow business,
including
individual buyers and sellers of properties,
may be affected in a

2.
The mayor must abstain from participating in specific
decisions as discussed below.
a.

decisions,

sections.facts:
From the information you have provided,
we have summarized
the facts which are relevant to your request.
City of Palm Springs Mayor Will Kleindienst is an architect
by occupation.
However,
Mr. Kleindienst has given up his
architectural practice.
The mayor’s wife is the sole owner of the
Escrow Connection,
an escrow company within the City of Palm
Springs.
The Escrow Connection is a community property asset.
The Escrow Connection’s fees are generated in accordance with
this formula:
$175 plus
$2 per $1,000 of the purchase price from


Our File No.
A—95--352
both the buyer and the seller
(for a $100,000 purchase price, fees
would be $375 for the buyer and $375 for the seller).
The escrow agent is not a broker.
The escrow agent does not
find the buyer or seller,
does not set the price of property,
and
does not negotiate the terms of the transaction.
Typically, the
escrow agent is recommended by one of the brokers.
The escrow
agent enters into
a contractual arrangement with the buyer and the
seller to carry out the terms of the contract between the buyer
and the seller,
as negotiated by those parties.
The city council,
with Mayor Kleindienst abstaining,
in May
1995 approved the Palm Springs Airport Master Plan,
Noise Study,
and Environmental Impact Report,
and made several decisions
regarding applications for federal funding for the Airport Project
(collectively the “Airport Project”).
Following this approval,
a
referendum petition was submitted,
and the matter of approval of
the Master Plan was placed on the November 7,
1995, ballot.
The
ballot measure was approved by the voters.
Future Airport Project decisions
3 will include:
(1)
settlement negotiations concerning the litigation challenging
approval of the Environmental Impact Report
(the “EIR”);
(2)
implementation of a multi—million dollar program to install
noise insulation for approximately 200 residences within the
65
decibel
CNEL 1999 noise contour around the airport;
and
(3) the
rezoning of certain property from Residential to Office!
Professional in response to the Noise Study recommendations.
Including the Escrow Connection, there are some 20 escrow
companies active within the Palm Springs/Cathedral City area.
A
total of 1,442 escrows closed in the Palm Springs/Cathedral City
area from August 1,
1994, to August 1,
1995.
Of this number,
750
or
52% were handled by the Escrow Connection.
The Escrow
Connection collected a gross total of $879,761 for the escrows.
A total of 5,043 properties are located within 2,500 feet of
the airport.
During the August 1,
1994
- August 1,
1995, period,
136 escrows closed within 2,500 feet of the airport.
During that
time, the Escrow Connection handled 36 or 26% of the escrows
within the 2,500 foot radius.
These airport area escrows
generated $31,299 in gross revenues for the Escrow Connection
during this period.
Of the Escrow Connection’s total Palm
Springs/Cathedral City area business,
3.13% stemmed from
properties within 2,500 feet of the airport.
You have inquired about decisions concerning the preparation of
a new master plan, if the master plan before the voters was
defeated.
The ballot measure was approved by the voters on
November 7,
1995.
Therefore, the issue is moot.


Our File No.
A—95—352
With respect to the effect of the Airport Project on real
property values, the EIR for the Airport Master Plan addressed the
subject in response to comments received suggesting that property
values would decline.
Although the EIR conceded that airports
with noise impacts can depress property values,
due to the
mitigation measures imposed on this project, the EIR forecast no
significant noise impacts resulting from the project.
Based upon
this lack of impact,
the EIR projected no change in property
values.
To look in more detail at the effect on property values of
the decision to approve the Airport Project, the city commissioned
an appraiser to specifically examine the effect on land values
within 2,500 feet of the airport
(the “Study Area”) before and
after the decision to expand the airport was announced.
The
announcement was made on May 17,
1995.
The appraiser analyzed
single-family residence sales during three time periods:
(1)
the
period from November 17,
1993, to October 17,
1994
(up to
6 months
prior to the announcement of the decision); November 18,
1994, to
May 17,
1995
(6 months prior to the decision;
and May 18,
1995, to
July 20,
1995
(2 months following the decision).
The appraiser
compared the Study Area to the city generally,
and compared it to
a control area with housing priced in the same category (“Control
Area”).
Among other things,
the study showed the following:
1.
For all sales,
between the first and second time
periods
(up to
6 months before the announcement of the decision),
average sale prices per square foot increased in the City of Palm
Springs by 24%,
decreased by
7% in the Study Area,
and decreased
by
10% in the Control Area.
Between the second and third periods
(from the date of the decision to
2 months later), there was a
2%
decrease in sales in the City of Palm Springs,
a
1% decrease in
the Study Area,
and a
16% decrease in the Control Area.
The appraisal report stated:
Examination of the results from the third time
period (5/15/95 to 7/20/95)
reveals a larger
decrease in average market prices in the control
area than the subject.
I recognize that the sample
size used to compute this decrease in the control
area is small because a smaller market is being
measured for only a brief time period.
There is
consequence in noticing that while both markets
moved downward the control area appears to have
[sic]
suffered more than the subject area.
If the announcement of the Airport Master Plan
were negatively influencing marJct values in the
Airport vicinity the average prices should be
decreasing more in the subject area than in the
control area.
The opposite is the case.


Our File No.
A—95—352
2.
For all sales in the Study Area,
excluding new home
sales
(5% of the total sales),
between the first and second time
periods, the average sale prices per square foot increased by 2%.
By contrast,
when “all sales” were analyzed there was a decrease
of
7% as shown above.
Between the second and third periods, there
was an increase of
1% for sales in the Study Area, also excluding
new home sales,
compared to a decrease of
1% when “all sales” in
the Study Area were analyzed.
The appraiser concluded that it
appears that the submarket (sales excluding new homes)
is stable
with respect to prices in the Study Area through the time periods
studied.
3.
A comparison of sales of property of more than $50,000
and less than $250,000 revealed that the average price per square
foot in the Study Area increased by
6% and decreased by
1% in the
City of Palm Springs between the first and second time periods.
Between the second and third time periods, the average price in
the Study Area increased by 2.7% and decreased by 3.6% in the City
of Palm Springs.
According to the analysis,
the appraiser opined that the
reason that the average price per square foot of subject submarket
increased while the average in the city submarket decreased is
that the subject submarket units were less expensive than those in
the city.
It was also the appraiser’s opinion that current market
conditions favor the sale of more affordable single family units.
He concluded that “for the times specified in this report,
that the announcement of the Airport Master Plan to expand is
having no measurable impact on the market values of single family
residences” within 2,500 feet of the Palm Springs Airport.
sections.analysis:
I.
Conflicts of Interests Generally
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making,
or using their official position to
influence governmental decisions in which an official knows or has
reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
Mayor
Kleindienst is a public official subject to Section 87100.
(Section 82048.)
The conflict-of-interest analysis under the Act is a four-
part test:
(1)
A public official must be participating in a
governmental decision,
(2)
and it must be reasonably foreseeable
that,
(3)
the decision will have a material financial effect,
(4) distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on
the official,
a member of the official’s immediate family, or on
any one of five statutorily identified economic interests of the
official.


Our File No.
A—95—352
A.
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that an official has an economic
interest in a decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if
the decision will affect the official,
or a member of the
official’s immediate family,
or:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.

QUESTION: 1.
May Mayor Kleindienst participate in decisions that may
affect his spouse’s escrow business and clients of the business?
2.
Do the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Act
prohibit Mayor Kleindienst from participating in the following
City of Palm Springs decisions:
a.
Settlement negotiations in litigation challenging the
Environmental Impact Report concerning the city’s airport;
b.
Implementation of a multi-million dollar program to
install noise insulation within approximately 200 residences
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
2
Councilmember Will Kleindienst was elected Mayor of the City of
Palm Springs on November 7,
1995.

(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-95—352
located within the
65 decibel
CNEL 1999 noise contour around the
city’s airport;
and
c.
Rezoning of certain property from Residential to Office!
Professional in response to the Noise Study recommendations.

CONCLUSION: 1.
Mayor Kleindienst may not participate in decisions in
which it is reasonably foreseeable that his spouse’s escrow
business or sources of income to the escrow business,
including
individual buyers and sellers of properties,
may be affected in a
material manner.
2.
The mayor must abstain from participating in specific
decisions as discussed below.
a.
It is reasonably foreseeable that some of the litigation
decisions,
including settlement negotiations, will have a
financial effect on the escrow business or sources of income to
the business.
Therefore, if this is the case, the mayor must
abstain from participating in litigation decisions if the business
or sources of income to the business will be materially affected
by the decisions.
This determination must be made on a
case—by—
case basis.
b.
It is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning
the selection of the homes to benefit from the noise insulation
program will have a financial effect on the property owners
selected.
Thus, the mayor must abstain from participating in
decisions concerning the selection process if sources of income to
the mayor will be materially affected by these decisions.
c.
Similarly, it is reasonably foreseeable that rezoning
decisions will have the effect of either increasing or decreasing
property values.
Therefore, the mayor must abstain from
participating in such decisions if the property owners who are
sources of income to the mayor will be materially affected by the
decisions.
